header.title: docker-compose up projects --build --force-recreate
header.description:
services:
  portfolio:
    image: travaux:curated
    volumes:
      - ./projects:/app/showcase:ro
    labels:
      - 'filter.enabled=true'
      - 'stack.visible=true'
filter.reset: docker system prune -af --volumes # reset: nuclear option
filter.none_stack: Error response from daemon: stack not found
filter.all: docker ps -a # show everything. even the dead.
filter.client: docker secret create client-work /dev/null # client work is classified
status.in_progress: STATUS: running (healthy) # or at least it claims to be
status.archived: STATUS: exited (137) OOMKilled # archived by the kernel
omnicard.longDescription:
services:
  omnicard:
    build:
      context: ./omnicard
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.card-engine
      args:
        - RULES_ENGINE=custom
        - COMPLEXITY=yes
    depends_on:
      game-logic:
        condition: service_healthy
      realtime-sync:
        condition: service_started
      state-manager:
        condition: service_healthy
    # 47 containers for a card game
    # 'microservices' they said
    # 'it'll be simpler' they said
    # each card effect is its own container
    # deploying a new card requires a helm chart update
omnicard.highlights.0:
FROM rules-engine:modular AS card-logic
# multi-stage build for game rules. 12 stages.
omnicard.highlights.1:
docker events --filter 'type=container' --format '{{.Status}}'
# game events ARE container events. effect triggers = container starts.
omnicard.highlights.2:
docker swarm init --advertise-addr 0.0.0.0
# multiplayer via docker swarm. each player is a node.
omnicard.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: container orchestration is basically game state management
omnicard.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: OOMKill during a boss fight is technically a game mechanic
omnicard.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: 'one container per card' doesn't scale past 40 cards
pvzf-translation-fr.longDescription:
services:
  translation-lead:
    image: pvzf-translator:fr-lead
    environment:
      - ROLE=lead
      - LANGUAGE=fr
      - QUALITY_BAR=high
    volumes:
      - translations-fr:/app/output
      - terminology-db:/app/terms:ro
    # the terminology database is a 47MB JSON file
    # mounted read-only because last time someone
    # wrote to it, 'bonjour' became 'bonjourno'
    # (not even Italian. just wrong.)
    deploy:
      replicas: 1  # there can be only one lead
pvzf-translation-fr.highlights.0: LABEL role=lead maintainer=charles # leadership via Docker labels
pvzf-translation-fr.highlights.1:
docker diff translation-container
# tracking every file change for terminology consistency
pvzf-translation-fr.highlights.2:
docker volume create --name contribution-tracker
# each contribution is a volume mount. disk is cheap. right?
pvzf-translation-fr.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: docker volumes don't have spell-check
pvzf-translation-fr.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: replicas=1 means 'single point of failure'. also known as 'the lead'.
pvzf-translation-fr.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: mounting terminology as read-only was the best decision in the project's history
portfolio.shortDescription:
FROM angular:20-ssr AS portfolio
LABEL description="immersive portfolio experience" version="over-engineered"
portfolio.longDescription:
# This portfolio's docker-compose.yml:
services:
  angular-ssr:
    build: .
    ports: ['4200:4200']
  gsap-animations:
    image: gsap:latest
    # animations run in their own container
    # because isolation matters
  tailwind-compiler:
    image: tailwind:4
    volumes: ['./styles:/app/styles']
    # tailwind in a container. watches files.
    # rebuilds on change. adds 30s to boot.
  audio-service:
    image: bloodborne-ost:ambient
    volumes: ['audio-state:/app/state']
    # the audio runs in a container
    # volume persistence for... volume level
    # yes that's a volume for volume
  seo-injector:
    image: meta-tags:dynamic
    depends_on: [angular-ssr, content-api]
# Total: 9 containers for a portfolio
# docker stats shows 14GB RAM usage
# 'but it's containerized' I whisper, crying
portfolio.highlights.0:
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
RUN npm ci && npm run build:ssr
# 'npm ci' takes 4 minutes. the alpine image didn't help.
portfolio.highlights.1:
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64
# multi-arch portfolio. in case someone views it on a Raspberry Pi.
portfolio.highlights.2:
docker run --gpus all gsap-renderer:latest
# GPU passthrough for CSS animations. necessary? no. cool? yes.
portfolio.highlights.3:
HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:4200 && lighthouse http://localhost:4200 --output json
# healthcheck runs Lighthouse. every 30 seconds. the CPU weeps.
portfolio.highlights.4:
LABEL design.system='custom' design.font='not-comic-sans' design.quality='high'
# design system defined as Docker labels. the metadata IS the documentation.
portfolio.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: 9 containers for a portfolio is 8 too many
portfolio.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: GPU passthrough for GSAP is technically impressive and morally questionable
portfolio.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: 'volume for volume' is the worst pun in DevOps history
portfolio.lessonsLearned.3: # Learned: multi-arch builds add 20 minutes for 0 users on ARM
lis-web.longDescription:
services:
  lis-web:
    image: professional-site:production
    environment:
      - CLIENT_SATISFACTION=pending
      - SCOPE_CREEP=enabled
      - BUDGET=it-depends
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.1'
          memory: 128M
    # limited resources because shared hosting
    # the container simulates shared hosting constraints
    # for authenticity
    # and also because the client won't pay for more
lis-web.highlights.0:
EXPOSE 80
# just port 80. no HTTPS. the client said 'the padlock is optional'.
lis-web.highlights.1: LABEL alignment='business-technical' # alignment tracked via label. always 'pending'.
lis-web.highlights.2: ENV PROJECT_TYPE=real # as opposed to our other projects which are... imaginary?
lis-web.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: client says 'simple site'. docker-compose.yml: 200 lines.
lis-web.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: scope_creep should not be an environment variable. it should be a feature flag. that's always ON.
lis-web.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: 128M memory limit teaches you what 'optimization' really means
dev-mates.longDescription:
# Dev-Mates Docker history:
# v1: docker run nginx -v ./index.html:/usr/share/nginx/html/
#     (entire site: 1 HTML file mounted into nginx)
# v2: added PHP-FPM container for contact form
#     (2 containers for a contact form. progress.)
# v3: migrated to WordPress
#     (docker-compose.yml: wordpress + mysql + phpmyadmin + redis + mailhog)
#     (5 containers for a company website)
# v4: ditched WordPress
#     (docker rmi wordpress --force. cathartic.)
# v5: Angular build in multi-stage Dockerfile
#     (3 stages, 1 output, 0 regrets)
# Current: 1 container. We went full circle.
dev-mates.highlights.1:
docker inspect dev-mates --format '{{.Config.Labels.coherence}}'
# coherence: tracked as a Docker label. value: 'mostly'.
dev-mates.highlights.2:
EXPOSE 443
# HTTPS enabled. we're credible now.
dev-mates.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: 5 containers for a company website is called 'enterprise architecture'
dev-mates.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: docker rmi wordpress --force is the most satisfying command in Docker
dev-mates.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: going from 5 containers to 1 is called 'maturity'
pvz-fuzion-console-manager.longDescripti:
# Translation checker as a Docker container:
# Step 1: docker build -t checker . (2 minutes)
# Step 2: docker run checker (3 seconds)
# Step 3: docker rmi checker (because disk)
# 
# The actual check: diff two JSON files.
# The Docker image: 800MB.
# Contains: Node.js, Python, Ruby, and Java.
# Uses: none of them. It's a bash script.
# The bash script: 4 lines.
# We dockerized a 4-line bash script.
# It has a Kubernetes Helm chart.
# For a 4-line bash script.
# We are engineers.
pvz-fuzion-console-manager.highlights.0:
docker run --rm checker diff /app/en.json /app/fr.json
# shelling out to diff inside a 800MB container
pvz-fuzion-console-manager.highlights.1:
docker logs checker --since 1h | grep MISSING
# quality control via grep on container logs
pvz-fuzion-console-manager.highlights.2:
docker stats checker --no-stream
# community impact measured in CPU usage
pvz-fuzion-console-manager.lessonsLearne: # Learned: not everything needs to be a container. but here we are.
# Learned: 800MB for a bash script is 'production-ready'
# Learned: a Helm chart for a 4-line script is 'Kubernetes-native'
shreksophone.longDescription:
# Shreksophone: the only container that matters.
#
# Dockerfile:
# FROM alpine:latest
# RUN apk add mpv
# COPY shrek_sax.mp4 /app/
# ENTRYPOINT ["mpv", "--fs", "--loop", "/app/shrek_sax.mp4"]
#
# That's it. That's the entire application.
# 4 lines. Perfect. No vulnerabilities.
# (Trivy scan: 0 CVEs. The cleanest image in the registry.)
# 
# It requires --privileged for fullscreen.
# It requires --device /dev/snd for audio.
# It requires /dev/video0 for... actually no.
# It just needs your faith.
#
# docker push shreksophone:latest has 47,000 pulls.
# It's our most popular image.
# The portfolio image has 3 pulls. All from CI.
shreksophone.highlights.0:
FROM alpine:latest
# minimal base image. maximum Shrek.
shreksophone.highlights.1:
ENTRYPOINT ["mpv", "--fs", "--loop"]
# fullscreen, infinite loop. like Shrek intended.
shreksophone.highlights.2:
docker scan shreksophone:latest
# 0 vulnerabilities. Shrek protects this image.
shreksophone.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: the simplest Dockerfile is the best Dockerfile
shreksophone.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: 47,000 pulls > any enterprise project on my registry
shreksophone.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: --privileged flag for Shrek is always justified
glossairequest.longDescription:
services:
  quiz-frontend:
    image: angular:quiz-ui
    ports: ['4200:4200']
  quiz-backend:
    image: aspnet:quiz-api
    ports: ['5000:5000']
    environment:
      - JWT_SECRET=changeme  # still says changeme in prod
      - DB_PASSWORD=also-changeme  # also still changeme
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=changeme  # sensing a pattern?
    volumes:
      - quiz-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
  # Every env var says 'changeme'
  # None were changed
  # The real quiz was the security holes we made along the way
glossairequest.highlights.1:
docker volume create quiz-scores
# scores in a volume. persistent. immutable. unchangeable. (we lost the volume name once. scores: gone.)
glossairequest.highlights.2:
docker network create --internal quiz-network
# internal network between front and back. security! (the DB port is still exposed. oops.)
glossairequest.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: 'changeme' in prod is not a password strategy. it's a prayer.
glossairequest.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: docker volume names should be documented somewhere other than 'it's the default one'
glossairequest.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: --internal network means nothing if you EXPOSE the DB port
league-of-data-base.longDescription:
services:
  riot-api-fetcher:
    image: api-fetcher:latest
    environment:
      - RIOT_API_KEY=RGAPI-xxxxxxxx  # rotates every 24h. we forget to update it every 24h.
    deploy:
      restart_policy:
        condition: on-failure
        delay: 60s  # rate limited? just wait.
        max_attempts: 9999  # never give up. never surrender.
  data-storage:
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - champion-data:/app/data  # 47GB of champion splash arts
      - item-data:/app/items     # 12GB of item icons
    # total disk usage: 'df -h' returns 'please stop'
  image-optimizer:
    image: sharp:latest
    # this container optimizes images
    # it has never run successfully
    # the entry in docker-compose is aspirational
league-of-data-base.highlights.0:
ENV RIOT_API_KEY=RGAPI-expired-3-days-ago
# the key rotation is 'someone remembers during standup'
league-of-data-base.highlights.1:
docker system df
# hard links save 60% disk space. the other 40% is Docker layers.
league-of-data-base.highlights.2:
docker run --rm -it league-data sh -c 'ls /app/public/*.jpg | wc -l'
# 14,847 images. responsive: no. present: very yes.
league-of-data-base.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: API key rotation should be automated, not 'standup-driven'
league-of-data-base.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: hard links in Docker layers is actually clever. we're proud of this one.
league-of-data-base.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: 'aspirational containers' in docker-compose is a new pattern we invented
blender-collection.longDescription:
services:
  blender-hub:
    image: blender-collection:latest
    volumes:
      - addons:/app/addons    # 200GB of Blender addons
      - uploads:/app/uploads   # user uploads. unvalidated. unchecked. unhinged.
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 32G  # the zip operation needs... a lot.
  worker:
    image: blender-worker:latest
    entrypoint: ['sh', '-c', 'while true; do process_queue; sleep 5; done']
    # the immortal worker. dockerized version.
    # restart: always + while true = double immortality
    restart: always
  admin-dashboard:
    image: admin:analytics
    environment:
      - QUERY_TIMEOUT=never  # the analytics query takes 30s. the timeout is... never.
    depends_on:
      - postgres
    # the dashboard container uses more CPU than the actual app
    # because COUNT(*) on 2M rows every page load
blender-collection.highlights.0:
docker exec blender-hub sh -c 'ls /app/uploads'
# user roles: checked via volume mount permissions. root = admin.
blender-collection.highlights.1:
docker stats admin-dashboard --no-stream
# CPU: 98%. Memory: 89%. Purpose: displaying a number.
blender-collection.highlights.2:
docker inspect worker --format '{{.State.StartedAt}}'
# started: 8 months ago. PID 1. unkillable. we've accepted it.
blender-collection.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: user uploads + no validation = docker exec surprise
blender-collection.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: COUNT(*) should be cached. not containerized.
blender-collection.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: restart:always + while true + docker = immortal process that survives everything including your will to live
symfony-session.longDescription:
services:
  symfony-app:
    image: symfony:session-manager
    volumes:
      - session-data:/var/lib/php/sessions  # 4MB per session. 10,000 users. do the math.
      - /tmp/sessions:/tmp/sessions          # overflow sessions go to /tmp. /tmp is 512MB. it fills up.
    environment:
      - SESSION_LIFETIME=forever  # not a valid PHP value but the container doesn't complain
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 64G  # 60GB of that is session data
  session-cleaner:
    image: alpine:latest
    entrypoint: ['sh', '-c', 'while true; do rm -rf /sessions/sess_*; sleep 604800; done']
    volumes:
      - session-data:/sessions
    # the session cleaner: runs weekly
    # deletes all sessions every Sunday night
    # Monday morning: 'why am I logged out?'
    # Jira ticket: 'WONTFIX - it\'s a feature'
symfony-session.highlights.0:
docker run --rm captcha-generator:comic-sans
# captcha generator. uses Comic Sans. bots solve it faster than humans.
symfony-session.highlights.1:
docker exec symfony-app php bin/console make:crud
# CRUD via artisan... wait, wrong framework. bin/console. same energy.
symfony-session.highlights.2:
docker run --rm pdf-generator:fpdf -v calendar:/app/output
# PDF generation container. 800MB image. generates one PDF. exits.
symfony-session.lessonsLearned.0: # Learned: SESSION_LIFETIME=forever is not valid but the container respected it anyway. terrifying.
symfony-session.lessonsLearned.1: # Learned: weekly session deletion is a 'feature' if you label the Jira ticket correctly
symfony-session.lessonsLearned.2: # Learned: 64GB memory limit for sessions means you've made several life choices
timeline.featured:
LABEL featured=true priority=critical
# featured projects get their own node in the swarm
modal.close: docker stop modal && docker rm modal # close with extreme prejudice
modal.previous: docker rollback project-viewer # previous = rollback. same thing.
modal.description: docker logs project --tail 50 # description = last 50 log lines
modal.lessons: docker history project:latest # lessons = layer history
modal.demo: docker run --rm -p 8080:80 demo:latest # demo: ephemeral container. gone when you close it.
modal.site: kubectl port-forward svc/project-site 8080:80 # we switched to k8s mid-sentence
today: docker exec timekeeper date +%Y-%m-%d # dedicated container for current date
